context: Current closing price of Mondelez (MDLZ): $59.00 USD as of 2026-04-10.
justification_text:
My point estimates across all three horizons reflect a view of modest upward drift from what appears to be a current level around 59. At the one-day horizon, the estimate of 59.3 is essentially anchored to the prevailing price or level, with only minimal expected movement reflecting typical short-run mean reversion and momentum tendencies. At the one-week horizon, the slight uptick to 59.5 reflects a weak positive drift assumption, consistent with a broadly neutral-to-slightly-bullish near-term environment. At the one-month horizon, the 60.0 estimate continues this gradual drift, representing my baseline expectation that conditions remain broadly stable without major structural disruptions.

The width of the confidence intervals grows substantially as the horizon extends, which reflects the natural accumulation of uncertainty over time. At one day, the 90% interval of 57.5 to 61.1 is relatively tight because short-run volatility is constrained and there are few scheduled high-impact events that could cause dramatic moves within a single session. By one week, the interval widens to 56.0–63.0, incorporating the possibility of unexpected macro data releases, central bank commentary, or geopolitical developments that could shift sentiment materially. At one month, the interval of 53.0–67.0 is quite wide, reflecting that over a four-week window there are many possible paths, including multiple data prints, policy signals, and shifts in market risk appetite that compound uncertainty considerably.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside these intervals include a sudden and large surprise in key macroeconomic data — such as an inflation print significantly above or below consensus — that forces rapid repricing. Central bank communications that deviate sharply from market expectations could similarly drive outsized moves. On the geopolitical side, an escalation in existing conflicts or emergence of a new shock could trigger risk-off behavior that pushes the lower bound well below 53. Conversely, a resolution of uncertainty or a surprisingly strong growth signal could push beyond 67 at the one-month horizon.

It is worth acknowledging that my confidence intervals, while calibrated to reflect 90% coverage in principle, are subject to model uncertainty and the known tendency of financial and economic variables to exhibit fat tails. Real-world distributions have more extreme outcomes than normal assumptions imply, meaning the true probability of exceeding these bounds may be somewhat higher than the stated 10% combined probability. Any regime change — a sudden liquidity crisis, a policy pivot, or an unexpected geopolitical event — represents tail risk that no interval width can fully capture.
